name: Wadd
description: Wadd was a prominent pre-Islamic Arabian deity, particularly revered by the Banu Dhuhl ibn Shayban tribe. He was considered a god of love, friendship, and compassion, embodying the virtues of kinship and social harmony. Depictions of Wadd are scarce, but he was often represented as a strong, benevolent male figure, symbolizing the importance of male lineage and leadership within the tribal structure. His worship likely involved rituals focused on strengthening bonds between individuals and families, promoting cooperation and mutual respect. Shrines dedicated to Wadd were places of gathering and celebration, where oaths were sworn and disputes were resolved. The decline of Wadd's worship coincided with the rise of Islam, as the new religion promoted monotheism and discouraged the veneration of multiple deities. However, the values associated with Wadd, such as love and friendship, continued to resonate within Arabian culture, albeit expressed through different religious and social frameworks. Some scholars believe that the concept of compassion and forgiveness in Islam may have been influenced by the pre-existing reverence for deities like Wadd, who embodied these qualities. Despite the lack of concrete historical evidence, Wadd remains a significant figure in understanding the complex religious landscape of pre-Islamic Arabia, representing a time when tribal loyalties and ancestral veneration played a central role in shaping the social and spiritual lives of the people.
subtitle: God of Love, Friendship, and Kinship

keyMyths.title: The Oath of Brotherhood
keyMyths.description: Two warring tribes swore an oath of brotherhood before Wadd's shrine, ending a long-standing feud and ushering in an era of peace. This act solidified Wadd's association with reconciliation and unity.

keyMyths.title: The Gift of Compassion
keyMyths.description: A cruel chieftain, known for his ruthlessness, prayed to Wadd and received the gift of compassion, transforming him into a benevolent ruler. This myth emphasizes Wadd's power to inspire positive change.

keyMyths.title: The Lost Friend
keyMyths.description: A man lost his dear friend but found him again by praying to Wadd. This reaffirmed his role as a deity of friendship and companionship.

sources.author: Ibn al-Kalbi
